mainmatter.p: The intrinsic path object on a type A, written A ^{=}, is defined by setting x \cong _{ A ^{=} } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.

frontmatter.title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
The intrinsic path object on a type A, written A ^{=}, is defined by setting x \cong _{ A ^{=} } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

Let A,B \colon \mathsf {PathObj} be two path objects. The binary product A \times B is equipped with the structure of a path object by setting \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A \times B} \mathopen {} \left ( u,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv x \cong _{A}u \times y \cong _{B}v and 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {refl} , \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {}.
The univalence property of A \times B follows immediately from that of A and B.

If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

mainmatter.p: Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g \colon A \to B. For any identification p \colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } \, p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \, p \cdot Hy.

By the univalence principle for functions (), we may consider just the case that f \equiv g and H \equiv 1_{ f }, and so we must show that \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl}. By identification induction, we may assume x \equiv y and p \equiv \mathsf {refl} to show \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl}, which is immediate.

frontmatter.title: Coherence for homotopies onto the identity

mainmatter.p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{ A } be a homotopy from f \colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H \circ f to \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ H.

By the univalence principle for functions (), we may consider only the case that f \equiv 1_{ A } and H \equiv 1_{ 1_{ A } } exhibit the identity homotopy 1_{ 1_{ 1_{ A } } } \colon 1_{ 1_{ A } } \sim 1_{ 1_{ A } }.

A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxy \cong G'xy that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} is a cocartesian family of path objects over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( Gxx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.

Suppose we have two centres of contraction a,a':A with \chi _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a = x and \chi '_ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a'=x as witnesses of contraction. To identify \mathopen {} \left ( a, \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} with \mathopen {} \left ( a', \chi '_ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}, we employ the covariant sum of each of the pointwise path object structures on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x over the intrinsic path object structure on A, constructing an identification p \colon a = a' followed by a homotopy H \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left ( \chi _{a'} \right ) \mathclose {} _*^{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x} \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \sim \chi '_ \bullet. Fixing z \colon A, we compute:

The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce as contractible types are propositions and propositions are sets.

Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is a cartesian family over A ^{=}:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} }} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic path object on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
